description: Rules targeting noun-phrase-level structures: pronoun types, determiner use, noun phrase patterns, adjective/adverb modification, and prepositional phrases. Detected via POS tag sequences.

examples:
There are n't many tickets. (other; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2010; Others; Pass)

I bought them because I have n't got many winter clothes. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Spanish - European; Pass)
structures.explanation: 'Not many' — negative quantifier for countable nouns expressing a small number (e.g. 'There aren't many people here').

examples:
I bought a new jacket because mine was very old. (Key English Test; A2; Italian)

I bought a pullover and trousers because mine are too small for me. (Key English Test; A2; Korean)

The phone is not m
structures.explanation: 'Mine' — first-person possessive pronoun replacing a noun phrase (e.g. 'That bag is mine').

examples:
But we didn't have much money, so we didn't buy many things. (Vietnam; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Vietnamese; Pass)

I want to sell the television because I haven't got much time to watch it. (France; A2 WAYS
structures.explanation: 'Not much' — negative quantifier for uncountable nouns expressing a small amount (e.g. 'There isn't much time').

examples:
[talking about a phone] I chose the black one with the numbers in orange. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Spanish - European; Pass)

I bought a new pullover, because my old one is too small. (Romania; A2 W
structures.explanation: 'One' — impersonal pronoun referring to people in general, or substituting for a previously mentioned noun (e.g. 'One never knows').

examples:
That was amazing. (Peru; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

After that, we can go and drink a cup of coffee and eat a piece of cake. (Mexico; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Spanish - Latin Americ

I also want to see that film. (Myanmar; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Burmese; Pass)

I know we have that course in our college. (A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Spanish - Other; Pass)
structures.explanation: 'That' as demonstrative pronoun — replaces a noun already mentioned (e.g. 'I prefer that').

examples:
I bought these clothes because they weren't expensive. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Spanish - European; Pass)

I bought these things because they were perfect. (Greece; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Greek; Pass)
structures.explanation: 'These' — plural demonstrative pointing to things nearby (e.g. 'These books are mine').

examples:
The cost of those clothes was £300. (Malaysia; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Chinese; Pass)

I love to play those instruments. (Sri Lanka; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2010; Singhalese; Pass)
structures.explanation: 'Those' — plural demonstrative pointing to things further away (e.g. 'Those shoes look great').

examples:
It was my best present, although I also loved yours. (Key English Test; A2; Spanish-European)

My favourite meal is spaghetti with garlic bread. What's yours? (Key English Test for Schools; A2; Tagalo
structures.explanation: 'Yours' as object — second-person possessive pronoun used as the object (e.g. 'I took yours by mistake').

examples:
You don't have to bring anything. (Italy; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2006; Italian; Pass)

When I want to eat something in the night, I can't find anything because it is too dark. (A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Polish; Pass)
structures.explanation: Negative 'anything' — 'not + anything' (or 'nothing') to express a complete absence (e.g. 'I don't know anything about it').

examples:
It was sunny and really hot every day! (Argentina; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

I'm so happy because we can have a party for my birthday together! (Italy; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Cro
structures.explanation: Adjective with degree adverb — intensifier or softener modifying an adjective (e.g. 'very big', 'quite interesting', 'rather cold').

examples:
After the concert we can have a pizza or something else. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2010; Spanish - European; Pass)

You do not have to bring any food, but I will pleased if you could bring something to dri
structures.explanation: 'Something' in vague language — refers to an unspecified thing or softens a statement (e.g. 'There's something about this place').

examples:
Every 5 minutes, he makes another great joke. (Germany; B1 THRESHOLD; 2001; German; Pass)

Maria cried, but after a few minutes, she dried her tears and said: "Tomorrow will be another day! (Peru; B1
structures.explanation: 'Another' — determiner meaning 'one more' or 'a different one' (e.g. 'Can I have another coffee?').

examples:
My pillows are getting old and I need new ones. (Brazil; B1 THRESHOLD; 2001; Portuguese; Pass)

To tell the truth, I prefer large schools to small ones. (Russia; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Russian; Pass)

We
structures.explanation: 'Ones' — pronoun substituting for a plural noun already mentioned (e.g. 'The red ones are better').

examples:
Hope you have a friend as good as mine! (Preliminary English Test for Schools; Malaysia; B1 THRESHOLD; 2009; Chinese; Pass)

My bedroom is not as big as yours but it is lovely. (Preliminary English Te
structures.explanation: Possessive pronoun in comparisons — 'mine'/'yours' inside a comparative clause (e.g. 'Her score is higher than mine').

examples:
Never have I seen such a realistic and impressive film before. (Argentina; B2 VANTAGE; 2008; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

Never had I thought that my parents would do this to me. (Netherlands; B2
structures.explanation: Fronted 'never' with inversion — 'never' at the front of a clause for emphasis, followed by auxiliary before subject (e.g. 'Never had I felt so tired').

examples:
At the entrance, the security guard asked for my identity card. Pat gave hers. (First Certificate in English; B2; French)

She didn't remember leaving it there, it wasn't even hers, which is what frig
structures.explanation: 'Hers' as object — third-person feminine possessive pronoun (e.g. 'I borrowed hers').

examples:
Can we rent windsurf boards or should we bring ours with us? (First Certificate in English; B2; Turkish)

[talking about characters in a book] … when we read about them we think that we are the charac
structures.explanation: 'Ours' as object — first-person plural possessive pronoun (e.g. 'They took ours by mistake').

examples:
I could show them the traditional things of our country and learn theirs. (Portugal; B2 VANTAGE; 1993; Portuguese; Pass)

She told him that this baby was theirs. (Germany; B2 VANTAGE; 1997; Turkish; P
structures.explanation: 'Theirs' as object — third-person plural possessive pronoun (e.g. 'We saw theirs first').

examples:
I told my mother that your Spanish omelette is better than hers. (First Certificate in English; B2; Spanish – European) 

Lots of changes which may occur during the next fifty years can make us fear t
structures.explanation: Possessive pronoun in comparisons — 'ours'/'hers' in a comparative structure (e.g. 'Their house is bigger than ours').

examples:
In my opinion, the main characters do not have to be liked by readers but the story itself must be good and interesting to be successful. (First Certificate in English; B2; Swiss German)

The building
structures.explanation: 'Itself' for emphasis — reflexive pronoun stressing that no one else is involved (e.g. 'The problem solved itself').

examples:
Dear Sir/Madam, I am writing to you in response to the advertisement published by yourselves in "the Traveller" regarding The Trip of a Lifetime. (First Certificate in English; B2; Portuguese)

The qu
structures.explanation: 'Yourselves' for politeness — reflexive used to address a group in a welcoming way (e.g. 'Please help yourselves').

examples: Second, we would draw your attention to the fact that neither party could foresee or predict the recent rise in the price of wheat. (Russia; C1 EFFECTIVE OPERATIONAL PROFICIENCY; 2007; Russian; Pass)
structures.explanation: 'Either'/'neither' with singular noun — used to choose between or exclude two options (e.g. 'Either option works', 'Neither side agreed').

examples:
Hardly had he reached his front door when he heard the phone ringing. (Greece; C2 MASTERY; 1993; Greek; Pass)

Hardly had she finished her complaints when Bob stood up, paid and left. (Switzerland; C2
structures.explanation: Fronted 'hardly' with inversion — negative adverb 'hardly' in initial position triggers subject-auxiliary inversion (e.g. 'Hardly had she left when it started raining').
